title: Classifiers (Measure Words)
description: Vietnamese uses classifiers (measure words) before nouns — similar to how English says 'a piece of paper' or 'a head of lettuce,' but for almost everything. Master the most common classifiers and which nouns they pair with.

sections.content:
In English, you can just say 'a cat' or 'two books.' In Vietnamese, you need a **classifier** (loại từ) between the number and the noun. Think of it like a label that categorizes what kind of thing you're talking about.

The pattern is: **Number + Classifier + Noun**
- một **con** mèo = one (animal) cat
- hai **cuốn** sách = two (volume) books
- ba **cái** áo = three (thing) shirts

You also use classifiers when pointing at things:
- **con** chó đó = that dog
- **cái** bàn này = this table

**The good news for heritage speakers:** You probably already use classifiers instinctively. You'd never say 'một mèo' — it sounds completely wrong to your ear. This lesson will help you formalize what you already feel and fill in gaps for classifiers you might not use often.

**The most important classifier is 'cái.'** It's the default/general classifier for inanimate objects. When in doubt, 'cái' is usually safe (though not always technically correct, people will understand you).

items.notes: The most common classifier. Used for most inanimate objects: cái bàn (table), cái ghế (chair), cái áo (shirt), cái ly (glass), cái điện thoại (phone). When in doubt, use 'cái' — it's the safest default.

items.english: classifier for animals and some objects
items.notes: Primary classifier for animals: con chó (dog), con mèo (cat), con gà (chicken). Also used for some non-animal things: con dao (knife), con đường (road), con mắt (eye), con sông (river). These are often long, winding, or animate-seeming things.
Used specifically for people: một người bạn (a friend), hai người đàn ông (two men), ba người phụ nữ (three women). Also doubles as the word for 'person' or 'people.'

items.english: classifier for vehicles, single items of a pair
items.notes: Used for vehicles: chiếc xe (car/vehicle), chiếc tàu (ship). Also for single items from a pair: chiếc giày (one shoe), chiếc đũa (one chopstick). And for some objects: chiếc nón (hat), chiếc cầu (bridge). Can sometimes overlap with 'cái.'

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Chiếc xe này của ai vậy?

items.english: classifier for books and bound volumes
items.notes: Used for books, notebooks, and anything bound: cuốn sách (book), cuốn vở (notebook), cuốn từ điển (dictionary). Also spelled/said as 'quyển' — both are correct and interchangeable. Southern speakers tend to use 'cuốn' more.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tui mới mua cuốn sách này.

items.notes: Used for flat, sheet-like things: tờ giấy (sheet of paper), tờ báo (newspaper), tờ tiền (banknote). Think of it as the classifier for anything thin and flat.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cho tui mượn tờ giấy đi.
exampleSentence.english: Let me borrow a sheet of paper.

items.notes: Southern Vietnamese uses 'ly' for drinking glasses and cups: ly nước (glass of water), ly cà phê (cup of coffee). Northern Vietnamese uses 'cốc' instead. This is a classic North/South split — if someone says 'cốc,' they're from the North.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cho tui một ly trà đá.
exampleSentence.english: Give me one glass of iced tea.

items.notes: Southern word for 'bowl' — used as both a classifier and the noun itself: tô phở (bowl of pho), tô bún (bowl of vermicelli). In Northern Vietnamese, the word is 'bát.' Another easy tell for North vs South dialect.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tui muốn một tô phở bò.
exampleSentence.english: I want one bowl of beef pho.

items.notes: Southern classifier for fruits: trái cam (orange), trái xoài (mango), trái dừa (coconut). Northern Vietnamese uses 'quả' instead. Both 'trái' and 'quả' also mean 'fruit' as a standalone noun.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Mua cho tui mấy trái xoài đi.

items.notes: Used for bottles: chai nước (bottle of water), chai bia (bottle of beer), chai nước mắm (bottle of fish sauce). Simple and consistent — if it's in a bottle, use 'chai.'

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Mua cho ba chai bia đi con.
exampleSentence.english: Buy dad a bottle of beer, child.

items.notes: Southern term for 'plate' — doubles as a classifier: dĩa cơm (plate of rice), dĩa gỏi cuốn (plate of spring rolls). Northern says 'đĩa' with a different pronunciation. Also functions as the word for the plate itself.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cho tui thêm một dĩa rau nha.
exampleSentence.english: Give me one more plate of vegetables.

items.notes: Used for things that come in pairs: đôi giày (pair of shoes), đôi đũa (pair of chopsticks), đôi mắt (pair of eyes). Think of 'đôi' whenever you'd say 'a pair of' in English.

exampleSentence.english: This pair of shoes is too expensive!

items.notes: Used for composed/written pieces: bài hát (song), bài học (lesson), bài báo (article), bài thi (exam). If it was written or composed, 'bài' is your classifier.

Used for trees and long, stick-like objects: cây viết (pen), cây kem (popsicle — Southern), cây cầu (bridge, sometimes). Also the standalone word for 'tree.' If the object is long and narrow, 'cây' often works.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cho tui mượn cây viết đi.

sections.content:
**Classifiers reveal where you're from.**

Some of the biggest North-South dialect differences are in classifiers and measure words:

| Southern | Northern | English |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **ly** | cốc | glass/cup |
| **tô** | bát | bowl |
| **trái** | quả | fruit/classifier for fruit |
| **dĩa** | đĩa | plate |
| **cây kem** | que kem | popsicle |

If you grew up in a Southern Vietnamese household, you probably use the Southern set instinctively. Don't second-guess yourself — both are correct, they're just regional. But be aware of the Northern versions so you understand speakers from Hanoi or textbooks that use Northern Vietnamese.

**Classifier Cheat Sheet:**

| Classifier | Used for | Examples |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **cái** | General objects | cái bàn, cái ghế, cái áo |
| **con** | Animals + some objects | con chó, con dao, con đường |
| **người** | People | người bạn, người yêu |
| **chiếc** | Vehicles, single items | chiếc xe, chiếc giày |
| **cuốn** | Books, bound items | cuốn sách, cuốn vở |
| **tờ** | Flat sheets | tờ giấy, tờ tiền |
| **ly** | Glasses (Southern) | ly nước, ly cà phê |
| **tô** | Bowls (Southern) | tô phở, tô bún |
| **trái** | Fruits (Southern) | trái cam, trái xoài |
| **chai** | Bottles | chai nước, chai bia |
| **đôi** | Pairs | đôi giày, đôi đũa |
| **bài** | Songs, lessons, articles | bài hát, bài học |
| **cây** | Trees, stick-like items | cây viết, cây cầu |

**Pro tip:** When you can't remember the right classifier, use **'cái'** as a fallback. Vietnamese people do this in casual speech all the time. 'Cái' is the duct tape of classifiers.

exercises.question: Which classifier would you use for 'con mèo' (cat)?
exercises.explanation: 'Con' is the classifier for animals. 'Con mèo' = cat, 'con chó' = dog, 'con gà' = chicken. Remember: 'con' is for living creatures (and a few special non-living things like knives and roads).

exercises.question: In Southern Vietnamese, how do you say 'a glass of water'?
exercises.explanation: 'Ly nước' is the Southern way to say 'a glass of water.' 'Cốc' is the Northern equivalent. 'Tô' is for bowls, and 'chai' is for bottles. This is one of the classic North-South vocabulary differences.

exercises.question: Which is the correct way to say 'two books' in Vietnamese?
exercises.explanation: 'Hai cuốn sách' is correct. 'Cuốn' (or 'quyển') is the classifier for books and bound volumes. The pattern is: number + classifier + noun. 'Hai sách' (without a classifier) sounds incomplete and unnatural.

exercises.question: Complete the sentence: 'Cho tui một _____ phở bò.' (Give me one bowl of beef pho.)
exercises.english: Give me one bowl of beef pho.
exercises.explanation: 'Tô' is the Southern Vietnamese classifier/word for 'bowl.' 'Một tô phở bò' = one bowl of beef pho. Northern Vietnamese would say 'bát' instead.

exercises.question: Type 'a pair of chopsticks' in Vietnamese.
exercises.explanation: 'Đôi đũa' — 'đôi' is the classifier for pairs, and 'đũa' means chopsticks. You can also say 'một đôi đũa' (one pair of chopsticks) for emphasis.
